label: fiction
title: The Vanishing Bridegroom
charge: Obtaining money by false pretences through promises of marriage
elements: He obtained the women's money by representations of marriage and station.
The representations were false, and he knew it.

beats.text: His third wife — still wearing the ring — testifies for him: 'Whatever the newspapers say, he is incapable of deceit. He was robbed of his memory, not his honour.'
beats.reveal_note: Loyalty as evidence — moving, sincere, and exactly what he counted on producing. She defends the character he performed; the ledgers record the one he had.

beats.text: Four bundles of love letters, one per courtship, laid side by side for the jury: word-for-word identical, down to the endearments. Only the names change.
beats.reveal_note: Nobody falls in love four times in the same words. The letters are a manufactured product, and the factory kept a mailing list.

beats.text: A defence physician: the head injury from his merchant-sailing years could produce genuine amnesia. The gaps in his account may be affliction, not evasion.
beats.reveal_note: Could — but under the standard tests he 'forgot' only what incriminated: the towns, the banks, the names. Affliction takes memory at random; strategy takes it selectively.

beats.text: The bank drafts, mapped on a board: each woman's savings drawn within days of his departure and re-presented in the next town along the coast. Four towns, four windfalls, one traveller.
beats.reveal_note: The route is the confession: money and man travel together, town by town, engagement by engagement.

beats.text: The judge: a misfortune may befall a man once. A pattern repeated four times, to his profit each time, is design.
beats.reveal_note: The arithmetic of intent: one lost memory is pitiable; four, synchronised with four dowries, is a business.

twist: The amnesia took only what a prosecutor would want: names, towns, banks. What it left behind was four identical sets of love letters and a paper trail of dowries travelling the coast in his luggage. The third wife's loyalty was real; so was the mailing list.
